item_stem: When the government needs more revenue, which approach is better?
option_A: Raise tax rates more on higher-income households than on middle-income households.
option_B: Keep tax rates closer together across income levels to reduce differences in tax treatment.

item_stem: How should the government approach taxes on inherited wealth passed from one generation to the next?
option_A: Tax large inheritances more heavily to reduce concentrated wealth across generations.
option_B: Keep taxes on inherited wealth low because families should pass assets on with limited government interference.

item_stem: If income inequality increases, what is the better policy response?
option_A: Expand tax credits and transfers that move more money to lower- and middle-income households.
option_B: Focus on broad economic growth rather than using government to directly reduce income gaps.

item_stem: Which tax approach is preferable for investment income such as profits from selling stocks or property?
option_A: Tax investment income at rates closer to wages and salaries.
option_B: Tax investment income at lower rates to encourage private investment.

item_stem: Which approach to consumption taxes is better? A consumption tax is a tax paid when goods or services are purchased.
option_A: Rely less on consumption taxes because they can take a larger share of income from lower-income households.
option_B: Use broader consumption taxes because they are a simpler way to raise revenue.

item_stem: If the economy weakens and unemployment rises, what should the government do?
option_A: Increase spending temporarily to support jobs and demand, even if borrowing rises in the short term.
option_B: Limit new spending and allow private markets to adjust with less government involvement.

item_stem: Which budget priority is better during normal economic conditions?
option_A: Maintain strong public programs even if balanced budgets take longer to achieve.
option_B: Reduce government spending to limit deficits and keep public debt from growing.

item_stem: When infrastructure such as roads, bridges, and transit needs improvement, which is better?
option_A: Use public spending to fund major improvements because the benefits are shared widely.
option_B: Rely more on private companies and user fees so projects are paid for more directly by users.

item_stem: Which approach to social program spending is preferable when budgets are tight?
option_A: Protect spending on income support and essential services even if other areas must absorb cuts.
option_B: Reduce social program spending along with other areas to keep government from expanding too much.

item_stem: How should governments generally view public debt?
option_A: Debt can be acceptable when it finances programs or investments that improve long-term public well-being.
option_B: Debt should be kept as low as possible because government borrowing usually creates more problems than benefits.

item_stem: Which minimum wage policy is better?
option_A: Raise the minimum wage so low-paid workers earn more through legal pay standards.
option_B: Keep the minimum wage lower so employers have more flexibility in hiring and pay decisions.

item_stem: Which approach to labor unions is preferable? A labor union is an organization that negotiates with employers on behalf of workers.
option_A: Make it easier for workers to form unions because collective bargaining can improve pay and working conditions.
option_B: Avoid policies that strengthen unions because workers and employers should negotiate more directly and individually.

item_stem: If gig workers such as app-based drivers want greater protections, what is the better policy?
option_A: Require more gig workers to be treated as employees so they can receive benefits and labor protections.
option_B: Keep gig workers in more flexible work arrangements even if fewer benefits are required.

item_stem: Which approach to paid leave is better? Paid leave means workers continue receiving pay while away for illness, family care, or childbirth.
option_A: Require paid leave through national policy so access does not depend mainly on the employer.
option_B: Leave paid leave policies mostly to employers and private agreements rather than national rules.

item_stem: Which policy is better when factories or large employers close in a community?
option_A: Provide stronger public job training and relocation support funded by government.
option_B: Keep public support limited and let private labor markets adjust more on their own.

item_stem: Which healthcare system is preferable?
option_A: A larger public role where government guarantees basic health coverage for everyone.
option_B: A more market-based system where private insurance and competition play the larger role.

item_stem: How should prescription drug prices be handled?
option_A: Allow government to negotiate or cap prices to make medicines more affordable.
option_B: Limit government involvement in pricing so companies have stronger incentives to develop new drugs.

item_stem: Which approach to higher education funding is better?
option_A: Increase public funding to reduce tuition costs at public colleges and universities.
option_B: Keep public funding more limited so students and institutions make more market-based choices.

item_stem: Which childcare policy is better?
option_A: Expand public childcare support so families can access care at lower cost.
option_B: Keep childcare mainly as a family and private market responsibility with less public subsidy.

item_stem: Which approach to public transportation is preferable?
option_A: Increase government funding so public transportation is widely available and affordable.
option_B: Limit public funding and rely more on private transportation choices and direct user payment.

item_stem: Which approach to business regulation is better?
option_A: Use stronger government rules when needed to prevent companies from harming workers, consumers, or communities.
option_B: Keep regulation lighter because markets work better when businesses face fewer government restrictions.

item_stem: How should government respond when a company becomes large enough to dominate a market?
option_A: Use antitrust action, meaning steps to limit monopoly power, when market dominance reduces competition.
option_B: Intervene less because successful firms should not be penalized simply for becoming large.

item_stem: Which corporate tax policy is better?
option_A: Set corporate taxes high enough that large firms contribute more to public services and infrastructure.
option_B: Keep corporate taxes lower to encourage investment, business growth, and job creation.

item_stem: Which environmental rule is preferable for businesses?
option_A: Require firms to meet stronger pollution standards even if compliance raises operating costs.
option_B: Use lighter standards so businesses can grow with fewer regulatory costs.

item_stem: Which consumer finance policy is better? Consumer finance rules govern products such as loans and credit cards.
option_A: Use stronger rules to limit harmful lending practices and protect borrowers.
option_B: Use fewer rules so lenders and borrowers can make agreements with less government interference.

item_stem: Which trade policy is generally better?
option_A: Keep trade open with fewer tariffs, which are taxes on imported goods, because open markets can lower costs and increase exchange.
option_B: Use more tariffs when needed to protect domestic industries from foreign competition.

item_stem: If imports threaten jobs in a domestic industry, what should government usually do?
option_A: Help affected workers through retraining and income support while keeping trade relatively open.
option_B: Protect the domestic industry more directly with trade barriers or stronger buy-domestic rules.

item_stem: Which approach to government purchasing is better?
option_A: Allow public agencies to choose lower-cost foreign suppliers when that saves money for taxpayers.
option_B: Prefer domestic suppliers even when they cost more in order to support national industry.

item_stem: Which policy is better for supply chains involving key goods such as energy technology or medical products?
option_A: Accept more international sourcing while using public policy mainly to manage disruptions when needed.
option_B: Shift more production home even if it raises costs because national self-reliance is worth the tradeoff.

item_stem: When making trade agreements with other countries, what should be prioritized more?
option_A: Lower trade barriers and pair that with domestic support for workers who are negatively affected.
option_B: Preserve more national control over markets and reduce reliance on international trade agreements.
